content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 11, 2017

Kara K. Ueda
Best Best & Krieger
500 Capitol Mall, Suite 1700
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-095

Dear Ms. Ueda:

You are the City Attorney for the City of Woodland and are seeking advice on behalf of the
City. This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note, the Fair Political Practices Commission does not
provide advice relating to past conduct. This letter should not be construed as assistance on any
conduct that may have already taken place. (See Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Additionally, we are
only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other
general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

Does City Manager Paul Navazio have a conflict of interest under the Act that prohibits him
from participating on behalf of the City in tax sharing agreement negotiations with Yolo County.

CONCLUSION

Yes, City Manager Paul Navazio has a conflict of interest and is prohibited from
participating in the tax sharing agreement negotiations because there is a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect upon his financial interest.

FACTS

Paul Navazio serves as the City Manager of the City of Woodland and has served in that
position since April of 2012. In his capacity as City Manager, Mr. Navazio is responsible for,
among other things, implementing the City Council's policy direction and managing the City's
employees. He is routinely involved in advising the City Council on broad policy matters and

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-095
Page No. 2

makes or assists in making, recommendations concerning specific development projects and
policies.

The City is currently in the process of preparing a comprehensive update to its General
Plan. The General Plan, as proposed to be amended, would label the agricultural land on Harry
Lorenzo Drive located in Yolo County as "SP-1," which is shorthand for a forthcoming Specific
Plan. SP-1 as proposed in the Draft General Plan would develop, pursuant to a specific plan, as
three sub-areas, referred to as "SP-1A, -1B, and -1C." The SP-1A and SP-1B sub-areas would
include mixed-use neighborhoods anchored by a research and technology business park. These
areas would have a mix of residential units as well as open space and recreation areas. SP-1A sub-
area encompasses 347 acres and is located on the eastern portion of SP-1, closest to Harry Lorenzo
Avenue, between State Route 113 and Spring Lake. SP-1C is envisioned to be primarily residential.
SP-1 would provide connectivity with the Spring Lake Master Plan Area and SP-1 would be able to
use existing roads, drainage, and water and sewer infrastructure previously built and sized to
accommodate development within the Spring Lake Master Plan Area.

The City does not yet have specific details about the SP-1 project, including its density,
footprint, location of specific proposed uses, or circulation patterns. But the project is anticipated to
include 1.5 million square feet of office space, 1,120 single-family residential units, and 480 multi-
family units. The City entitlements and approvals anticipated for this project include: execution of a
tax-sharing agreement with the County of Yolo prior to unnexation of the land into the City,
approval of an environmental document, approval of a specific plan, approval of a development
agreement, amendment to the Zoning Code and approval of a subdivision map.

Mr. Navazio has recently purchased a single-family residence located in the Spring Lake
Master Plan Area of the City of Woodland and plans to relocate there. The residence that
Mr. Navazio has purchased is in the Arabella subdivision of Spring Lake. The residence is located
across the street from the proposed location of SP-1A and within 500 feet of its outermost
boundary. The city limits currently extend to Harry Lorenzo Drive and agricultural land in the
County of Yolo is on the other side of Harry Lorenzo Drive.

The City of Woodland and the County of Yolo have been negotiating a "tax sharing
agreement," pursuant to Revenue & Taxation Code Section 99, to determine the allocation of
property tax revenue between the City and the County upon annexation of land located in the
County to the City. The City anticipates annexation of several properties soon, and one of the tax
sharing agreements under negotiation is for SP-1. Mr. Navazio has been the primary negotiator on
behalf of the City. There have been no substantive negotiations between the City and County since
Mr. Navazio began the home purchasing process. However, during this time Mr. Navazio has
participated in brief contacts with the County related to procedural items such as which party was
preparing the first draft of the agreement, when it would be completed by and the timing for
consideration by both bodies. These were brief conversations that did not involve any substantive or
policy information that would influence the tax sharing agreement.

Entering into a tax sharing agreement is a prerequisite to having property currently located
in the County annexed to the City and annexation is required for the project to move forward. The
City and County must agree to an apportionment of revenues between the two different jurisdictions
as the County would be losing land that it had been previously deriving tax revenues from. Tax

File No. A-17-095
Page No. 3

sharing agreements do not change the intensity, layout, design or amenities located within the
development.

You are seeking guidance as to whether Mr. Navazio has a conflict of interest in
participating the tux sharing agreement negotiations between the City and the County due to his
financial interest in the residence located within 500 feet of the project.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A conflict of interest may arise only when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on the official or his or her immediate family that is distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.)

At issue here is Mr. Navazio's financial interest in the residential real property that he has
purchased located within 500 feet of SP-1A.

Reasonably Foreseeable: For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in a decision (such
as Mr. Navazio's property), Regulation 18701(b) states that a financial effect can be reasonably
foreseeable where it is a realistic possibility under ordinary circumstances.

Material Financial Effect: Regulation 18702.2(a) sets the materiality standard for real
property interests and provides a list of circumstances to consider. As relevant to your facts, the
financial effect will be material if the decisions:

"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially
altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking,
of property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial
interest;

"(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property...
Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice
allowing an official to participate under these circumstances if the Commission
determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no
reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property; or

"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration
under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a
nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of
the official's property."

In this case, if a tax sharing agreement is not reached, the project will not be able to move
forward at all, therefore the tax sharing agreement is a crucial part of the entire project.

File No. A-17-095
Page No. 4

While the City does not yet have specific details about the project, including its density, footprint, location of specific proposed uses, or circulation patterns, this is a large project anticipated to include 1.5 million square feet of office space, 1,120 single-family residential units, and 480 multi-family units. SP-1 is planned to use existing roads, drainage, and water and sewer infrastructure previously built and sized to accommodate development within the Spring Lake Master Plan Area, an area in which Mr. Navazio has purchased a home and plans to reside. Therefore, it is likely that this large-scale project that is located across the street from Mr. Navazio's real property will change the character of his property due to the proximity of the project to his new home. The traffic levels, privacy and noise levels will likely be affected as SP-1 would be using the existing roads that serve Mr. Navazio's real property.

The decisions related to the tax sharing agreement will affect real property values for properties located within 500 feet of Mr. Navazio's real property as well and a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration would understand that this large-scale project would influence the market value of Mr. Navazio's home. For these reasons, there is a reasonably foreseeable material financial affect upon Mr. Navazio's financial interest and he is prohibited from participating in the negotiations concerning the tax sharing agreement between the City and the County.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack Woodside
General Counsel

By: [signature]
Sukhi K. Brur
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

sections.question:
Does City Manager Paul Navazio have a conflict of interest under the Act that prohibits him 
from participating on behalf of the City in tax sharing agreement negotiations with Yolo County.
sections.conclusion:
Yes, City Manager Paul Navazio has a conflict of interest and is prohibited from 
participating in the tax sharing agreement negotiations because there is a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect upon his financial interest.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does City Manager Paul Navazio have a conflict of interest under the Act that prohibits him 
from participating on behalf of the City in tax sharing agreement negotiations with Yolo County.

CONCLUSION: Yes, City Manager Paul Navazio has a conflict of interest and is prohibited from 
participating in the tax sharing agreement negotiations because there is a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect upon his financial interest.
